posts.cooked:
I am trying to find a book that best lays out/tells the story of the period in time in Napa and others parts of California from say 1950-the 80s when the true classic wines of California were being made and discovered. Any recommendations on a book that covers the period that anyone would recommend? Really looking to learn more about some of the great winemakers from that era and where they were and what they did.

Thanks in advance!

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: There was a huge, doorstop of a book on California wine published by the University of California Press back in the late 70s or 80s. It was an excellent collection of articles that might be up your alley. I can’t find it quickly on line, but I’ve got it at home and can post the name if someone else hasn’t first.

posts.name: Bill Tex Landreth
posts.cooked: Hit up Richard Walker.

posts.name: C 0 R E Y M.
posts.cooked:
It’s not comprehensive, but George Tabor’s book on the Judgment of Paris covers a lot of this ground.

Also, not a book, but there’s a lot of really interesting material on the early days of the CA wine industry at the California Wine Industry Oral History Project: Bancroft Library | UC Berkeley Library

posts.name: Morgan Twain-Peterson
posts.cooked: If you want to find a book regarding the wines of California made in the 1880’s and 1890’s dig up George Hussman’s 1888 treatise- a pretty amazing read and remarkably prescient. The UC Davis tome from the early 1980’s is pretty fascinating; there is a nice piece by Paul Draper on Zinfandel.

posts.name: Aaron Nix-Gomez
posts.cooked: If you are looking at 1950-1980 then you must read James T. Lapsley’s “Bottled Poetry, Napa Winemaking from Prohibition to the Modern Era” as well as Charles L. Sullivan’s “Napa Wine A History”. These cover your period of interest plus a little bit more. To understand what was going on right before your period look at Frank Schoonmaker’s and Tom Marvel’s “American Wines” published in 1941.

posts.name: Aaron Nix-Gomez
posts.cooked: Morgan, to add to your suggestion for the 1880’s and 1890’s I recommend Vincent P. Carosso’s “The California Wine Industry, 1830-1890”. It has tons of 19th century references and a great bibliography. It is more of a history but it help frames all of the highly detailed viticultural reports where you can read about actual efforts in vineyards and wineries.

posts.name: Phil Carpenter
posts.cooked: This one is fun…done in 1973. Great profile on Heitz, etc…

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

The book I was thinking of was University of California/Sotheby’s Book of California Wine, which is still available: Amazon.com

Nate – Looking at your original query again, I think you should definitely check this out, particularly since it’s still in print and fairly cheap. There 44 articles by people like Paul Draper, Zelma Long, Gerald Asher. It covers history, winemaking, grape types, and many of the articles talk about the evolution of California wine. (There are some amusing passages talking about overripe wines in the 70s and the move back toward balance. Plus ca change…) I think you might find it captures a lot of the thought of that period. (It was published in 1984.)

John Morris:

(There are some amusing passages talking about overripe wines in the 70s and the move back toward balance. Plus ca change…) I think you might find it captures a lot of the thought of that period. (It was published in 1984.)

John thanks I am going to grab a copy of this from the local library and give it a look through.

posts.name: Nate Simon
posts.cooked: James Halliday’s “Wine Atlas of California” has some nice historical info.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Others here have stole my thunder, but here’s what we have in our library:

) Creating wine : the emergence of a world industry, 1840-1914 / James Simpson
(mostly Europe, but a chapter on CA)

) Guide to California wines. Introd. by Joseph Henry Jackson, John Melville (1955)

) The story of wine in California / Fisher, M. F. K. (1962)

) The taste of place : a cultural journey into terroir / Amy B. Trubek
[more about local food, but could be a fun read]

) Vineyards & wineries of California / author, Donald Holtgrieve, California State University, Chico ; production, Allan Cartography ; design, Stuart Allan ; cartographer, Pat Tamarin [map, but may have info you seek]

) Webster’s wine tours : California, Oregon, and Washington / Bob Thompson

) The Wine spectator’s wine country guide to California

) Winemaking in California : the account in words and pictures of the Golden State’s two-century-long adventure with wine / Ruth Teiser and Catherine Harroun

and don’t forget about The ZIN!:

) Zinfandel : a history of a grape and its wine / Charles L. Sullivan ; foreword by Paul Draper

posts.name: Eric LaMasters
posts.cooked: North American Pinot Noir by John Winthrop Haeger has some very good historical detail relating to Pinot Noir in California.

posts.name: Vincent Fritzsche
posts.cooked:
Another vote for Great Winemakers of California. Incredibly good interviews with pretty much everyone in the 1970s talking about everything from grape growing to cellar practices. You’d be amazed at how much of the conversation hasn’t changed. Highly recommended.

http://www.amazon.com/Great-Winemakers-California-Robert-Benson/dp/B000OPHZJA

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
The book Amerine wrote with Roessler is basically a book on how to analyze the statistics of wine tasting results etc. For those of you with insomnia…
Hurst Hannum became a professor of International law at Tufts. Bob Blumberg was/is a physician at Kaiser and quite active in Medical Friends of Wine here. Back in the early '70s they, along with Bruce Neyers and the late Jim Olsen, started a series of classes at UC Extension here that have turned into a small industry.

I concur about the Lapsley book. It’s excellent on the period from the end of Prohibition to ‘modern’ times.

Dr. Richard ‘Dick’ Peterson’s autobiography talks about his life at Gallo, BV, Taylor of California. I enjoyed it. There are some who quibble about his memories of life at Gallo.

Speaking of Gallo, what about Blood and Wine by Ellen Hawkes. This can be hard to find.

Frances Dinkelspiel wrote a book called Tangled Vines. It is somewhat personal but there is a lot about the history of winemaking in Southern California. I also recommend her bookm on the Hellman family, her cousins.

Did anybody recommend David Darlington’s books??He is a terrific writer and I have even read his book on Area 51.His book on zin is great and I greatly enjoyed An Ideal Wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:

The book Amerine wrote with Roessler is basically a book on how to analyze the statistics of wine tasting results etc.

I recently found this paper on the [u]Hilgardia[/u] archives website:

UC ANR
Hilgardia Vol. 30, No. 19
“Small Panel Sensory Evaluations of Wines by Scoring”
by C.S. Ough & G.A. Baker
May 1961


The paper’s online abstract features all cited sources, including related publications by Amerine, Baker, Roessler, and others.


Did anybody recommend David Darlington’s books??He is a terrific writer and I have even read his book on Area 51.His book on zin is great and I greatly enjoyed An Ideal Wine.

I love David Darlington’s style of writing. Angels’ Visits/Zinfandel and his articles in Wine & Spirits are enjoyable, educational reads.

The Sonoma Library’s “Winemaker Oral Histories” include dozens of historically significant grape growers and vintners from the region.


Sonoma Library
Sonoma County Winemaker Oral Histories at the Wine Library
Winemaker Oral Histories

“The Friends of the Wine Library (formerly known as the Wine Library Associates), the Winegrowers of Dry Creek Valley (formerly known as the Dry Creek Vintners’ Association) and others have produced oral histories from notable figures in the Sonoma County wine industry. Some have been digitized and are available through the Speaking of Wine Collection, but the remainder can be found at the Sonoma County Wine Library.”



The UC Berkeley Bancroft Library has many resources associated with California’s wine history. It seems that this website is difficult to navigate unless one has an author, publication title, or a very specific subject in mind.


I was able to locate an interview with Brother Timothy of Chateau LaSalle and Christian Brothers by first conducting a Google search engine using the “Books” tool.

Next, I accessed the Bancroft Library webpage using details taken from the Google Books results.


“Brother Timothy: The Christian Brothers as Winemakers”· Creator:

  • Timothy, Brother, interviewee
  • Teiser, Ruth, interviewer

· Published: Berkeley, CA, Regional Oral History Office, 1974

· Link to Content: Listen to Audio Excerpt

· Full Collection Name: Food and Agriculture Oral Histories Individual Interviews

· Subject (Topic): Natural Resources, Land Use, and the Environment

· Type: Text

· Format: Oral histories

· Archive: The Bancroft Library Oral History Center

· Note: Timothy, Brother. “Brother Timothy: The Christian Brothers as Winemakers.” Interview by Ruth Teiser in 1971 and 1973. Oral History Center, The Bancroft Library, University of California, Berkeley, 1974.

· Interview date(s): 1971
If you would prefer to listen to the interview of Brother Timothy, the Internet Archive has many Regional Oral History Office audio recordings that one can stream.


Additional Resource on the History of Christian Brothers:

The Catholic Northwest Progress, Vol 71, # 9
“Christian Brothers in the West”
March 7, 1968


Another fascinating read is the archived transcript of Joseph Phelps. His role in stimulating modern interest in California Rhône wine varieties cannot be overstated.

by Joseph Phelps

posts.name: Peter Petersen
posts.cooked: Dick Peterson’s book is a very interesting read from a much more personal perspective.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked:
Napa: The Story of an American Eden by James Conaway is a nice read.

This. It is a real tell all book in some respects. The history of the Mondavi family struggles are the stuff of legend and capture the spirit of the 80’s and 90’s well. The Valley is no stranger to family dynasties and scandal even going back to the 19th century. Gives a good history of the politics and maneuverings behind the ag preserve legislation. You will never look at the Napa Valley the same way again after this read.

This one is fun…done in 1973. Great profile on Heitz, etc…

I have a couple of copies of this one and another book, painstakingly assembled (exhaustive) list of wines from the old days with prices for some investment bank, I recall.

posts.name: Roy Piper

This is a great thread!

One more. Although mostly about soils in Napa, the last couple chapters in The Winemaker’s Dance are about various winemaking styles and is quite lucid.

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked:
I purchased this book a couple of years ago. Its focus is not exclusively devoted to California winemaking per se; however, I think this is a concise, well written text on the history and the (basic) science of a commonly used medium of transportation and storage.


University of Chicago Press
Wood, Whiskey and Wine: A History of Barrels (2014)
by Henry H. Work
Published by Reaktion Books, Chicago (224 pages)


"Barrels - we rarely acknowledge their importance, but without them we would be missing out on some of the world’s finest beverages - most notably whiskies and wines - and of course for over two thousand years they’ve been used to store, transport, and age an incredibly diverse array of provisions around the globe. In this comprehensive and wide-ranging book, Henry Work tells the intriguing story of the significant and ever-evolving role wooden barrels have played during the last two millennia, revealing how the history of the barrel parallels that of technology at large.

"Exploring how barrels adapted to the requirements of the world’s changing economy, Work journeys back to the barrel’s initial development, describing how the Celtic tribes of Northern Europe first crafted them in the first millennia BCE. He shows how barrels became intrinsically linked to the use of wood and ships and grew into a vital and flexible component of the shipping industry, used to transport not only wine and beer, but also nails, explosives, and even Tabasco sauce. Going beyond the shipping of goods, Work discusses the many uses of this cylindrical container and its relations - including its smaller cousin, the keg - and examines the process of aging different types of alcohol. He also looks at how barrels have survived under threat from today’s plastics, cardboards, and metals.

“Offering a new way of thinking about one of the most enduring and successful products in history, Wood, Whiskey and Wine will be a must-read for everyone from technology buffs to beverage aficionados who wish to better understand that evasive depth of flavor.”


860×1347 578 KB




UC Press
Gastronomica, Vol. 15, No. 4, (pp.99–100)
“Review: [u]Wood, Whiskey and Wine: A History of Barrels[/u], by Henry H. Work” __
by Bryce T. Bauer (2015)

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked:
Frequently, there is a dearth of in-depth information available online related to the history of West Coast wine industry and viticulture (c.1900-1970).


Per a published interview between Betty & Roy Brady, Gail Unzelman, and Bo Simons, UC Library’s Wine Writers Collection acquired William Heintz’s personal wine research, all scrawled onto countless notecards. It was stated that no efforts had been taken to make this trove of information more accessible to others.

According to Bo Simons, Bill Heintz had upwards of 10,000 notecards:

“…It is practically a good working index to The Napa Register, The St. Helena Star, and The Healdsburg Tribune, and some of the other papers up there, at least for wine information.”

At the time of the conversation no plans were made, nor foreseen, to make microfiche copies or anything of the like.


Today, the Online Archives of California lists many of the notes/materials donated to UC Library by wine authors and journalists, yet most are unavailable for viewing online. Only an in-person visit to the library would permit one to survey these treasures of wine history.

Of course, I am not speaking only of William Heintz. Many other well-known writers have donated their research notes to the library:

Leon David Adams

Gerald Asher

Anthony Dias Blue

Sukari and Shomari Bowman

Roy Brady

Katie Ballou Calhoun

Ruth Ellen Church

James Conaway

John Winthrop Haeger

Steve Heimoff

William E. Massee

Terry Robards

Jancis Robinson

Steven Spurrier

Charles Sullivan

Bob Thompson

Gail Unzelman

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked:
The archive of downloadable FPS newsletters provides a list of topics covered in each issue to guide readers’ selections.

As explained above, the Grape Newsletter did not come into its own as a separate publication until 1996. Prior to then, articles about wine, grapes, and viticulture were printed alongside works focusing on other UC Davis plant sciences.



UC Davis - Foundation Plant Services
Foundation Plant Services Grape Newsletters


"A rich source of historical detail about Foundation Plant Services (FPMS/FPS) can be found in annual publications produced by FPS staff from 1981 through 2012. ‘Real time coverage’ of important issues and events were reported for FPS customers and other interested parties.

"For a period between 1981 and 1995, FPS produced a single annual publication covering all FPS crops. In January of 1996, a separate newsletter for each crop was initiated. The grape program newsletter offered features on new clones and varieties in the FPS foundation collection, new disease testing and treatment technologies, seminars and other educational events for industry members, and reports of key events and people at FPS.

“The publications below include the all-crop newsletters for 1981-1995 and the grape program newsletters from 1996-2012.”


Sample Issue: “November 2007” (PDF Download):
“FPS Offers New Grape Selections for 2007-2008”

“Comments from the Director”

“Virus Status Update on FPS Source Vines and Selections”

“New Releases of Fay Triplett Red Wine Varieties ‘Maxine Rouge’ and ‘Rougett’”

“The Light Brown Apple Moth Invasion: Should Grape Growers Be Concerned?”

“The Zinfandels of FPS”

“Summary of FPS Zinfandel and Primitivo Selections; Their Sources, Status and Where They Are Available”

“Chardonnay History and Selections at FPS”

“Summary of FPS Chardonnay selections; Their Sources, Status and Where They Are Available”

“New Draft of Regulations for the California Registration and Certification Program for Grapevines is Ready for Review”
Foundation Plant Services Grape Newsletters:
https://fps.ucdavis.edu/grapenewsletters.cfm

posts.name: Mark Christenson
posts.cooked:
Maybe not exactly what the OP is looking for, but seeing the above references to Roy Brady reminded me of “The Brady Book”. Really enjoyable, meandering musings about California wine over a fairly broad period of time.

Not cheap, but very enjoyable if you can find a copy (published by Nomis but they are sold out).
